serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of Nyonya Otah Cutlet Rice weighing approximately 450 g, comprising otah cutlet, quinoa multigrain rice, french fries, mixed salad, and brown sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — otah (grilled spiced fish cake), cooked multigrain rice, french fries, mixed salad, brown sauce
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB Foodo) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Nyonya Otah Cutlet Rice as a composite dish. Values are estimated by component-level aggregation: HPB Foodo data for otah (grilled fish cake, ~120 g) and cooked multigrain rice (~150 g); USDA SR Legacy for french fries (~80 g) and mixed salad (~60 g); brown sauce (~40 g) estimated from USDA generic brown gravy. Per-100g values are derived from the weighted composite of the 450 g plate. Confidence is medium due to estimation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed rice dishes and analogous composite meals — University of Sydney GI Database; multigrain rice GI reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database | Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Nyonya Otah Cutlet Rice. GI estimated at 52 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: quinoa multigrain rice (GI ~50–54 per University of Sydney GI Database for multigrain/mixed grain rice). The protein and fat content of otah and the fibre from salad and multigrain rice further moderate the overall GI. Composite GI of 52 is defensible as a low-GI estimate. GL per serving = (52 × 91.8 g available carbohydrate per 450 g serving) / 100 = 47.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~310 mg per 100 g; ~1395 mg per 450 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: A single serving of Nyonya Otah Cutlet Rice provides approximately 1395 mg sodium, representing over 60% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals; portion control and requesting reduced brown sauce are advised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~47.7 per serving) despite low GI (52) due to large carbohydrate portion from multigrain rice and fries
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although the GI of this dish is low (52) owing to the multigrain rice and protein from otah, the total glycemic load per serving is moderate-to-high at approximately 47.7; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion of rice and fries and monitor postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~1.8 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium from brown sauce and otah seasoning
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains moderate saturated fat from coconut milk in the otah and contributes significant sodium per serving; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and request sauce on the side to reduce sodium and saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Nyonya Otah Cutlet Rice is a Singaporean Peranakan fusion dish that combines the traditional Southeast Asian grilled spiced fish cake (otah) in a modern cutlet presentation with quinoa multigrain rice, french fries, mixed salad, and brown sauce. Otah (also spelled otak-otak) is a classic Peranakan and Malay preparation made from minced or blended fish — typically mackerel or Spanish mackerel — mixed with a spiced coconut milk paste containing lemongrass, galangal, chilli, turmeric, and kaffir lime leaves, traditionally wrapped in banana leaf and grilled over charcoal. In this restaurant adaptation, the otah is formed into a cutlet shape and pan-fried or grilled, giving it a firmer texture and a slightly caramelised exterior. The quinoa multigrain rice provides a nutty, slightly chewy base with a lower glycemic index than plain white rice, making the dish moderately suitable for glycemic management. The french fries add a contrasting crispy texture, while the mixed salad contributes freshness and dietary fibre. The brown sauce ties the East-West elements together with a savoury, umami-rich flavour. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, with 8.2 g protein, 6.1 g fat, and 20.4 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is elevated (~310 mg per 100 g) due to the seasoned otah and brown sauce, making it a moderate concern for hypertensive individuals. The overall GI is estimated at 52 (low), but the glycemic load per full serving is moderate-to-high due to the combined carbohydrate from rice and fries. This dish is suitable for halal diets and is dairy-free. It is served at Flavours by Saute at Funan Mall, Singapore, and represents the East-meets-West culinary philosophy of modern Peranakan cuisine.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Nyonya Otah Cutlet Rice as a complete composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by component-level aggregation using HPB Singapore Foodo (otah, multigrain rice) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (fries, salad, brown sauce). GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (multigrain rice) per the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium due to the estimation methodology and the absence of a direct match in any primary approved source.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-level estimates; a direct laboratory analysis or HPB Foodo entry for this specific dish would improve confidence to high. 2. Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reliably reported for any of the component foods in the approved sources used. 3. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA SR Legacy entries used for the component estimation. 4. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the component-level sources used. 5. GI is estimated at 52 based on the multigrain rice component as the dominant carbohydrate source; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. The dish is served at Flavours by Saute, Funan Mall, Singapore (field survey data listing). 7. Trans fat is estimated at 0.1 g per 100 g from the french fries component; this is a trace amount. 8. Halal status is assumed based on the restaurant context and ingredient profile; formal halal certification should be verified with the restaurant.
